1.exposition: Chapter six proved the believer dead to sin; now Paul proves the believer dead to the law, and he argues it from a courtroom maxim every reader grants: law binds only the living (v.1). A married woman is bound to her husband while he lives, but his death frees her to marry another without reproach (vv.2–3). The application is deft, even if the figure bends: it is not the law that died but the believer, who died with Christ and so passed out of the law's jurisdiction, to belong to another — the risen Lord — that this new marriage might bear fruit for God (v.4). The old life in the flesh bore only fruit for death, its sinful passions actually stirred up by the very law that forbade them (v.5); but now, released by that death, the believer serves in the newness of the Spirit and not the oldness of the letter (v.6).
1.original_language: The analogy is worked in legal Greek. The wife is the hypandros gynē (“the woman under a man,” the technical term for a married woman) who is dedetai (“bound,” a perfect tense — a standing, settled obligation) to her husband while he lives. In v.6 the believer is katērgēthēmen apo tou nomou (“we were discharged from the law,” released as from a debt or a legal claim). The closing antithesis is untranslatable in a word: service en kainotēti pneumatos (“in newness of Spirit”) over against palaiotēti grammatos (“oldness of the letter”) — the living inward power of the Spirit against the dead external code.
1.historical_context: Paul writes “to those who know the law” (v.1) — a congregation steeped in Torah, for whom the marriage bond and its dissolution by death were legal commonplaces. The argument presupposes the death-with-Christ already established in Romans 6:3–4: that is the death which terminates the law-bond. The phrase “sinful passions aroused by the law” (v.5) is a promissory note Paul will redeem in 7:7–25, and “newness of the Spirit” (v.6) reaches forward into the full pneumatology of Romans 8.
1.christ: The christological hinge is v.4: the believer belongs “to him who was raised from the dead.” The death that frees is death-with-Christ; the new life is life joined to the risen one, and the “fruit for God” is nothing less than the resurrection life of Christ now bearing in those united to him. The shift from “fruit for death” (v.5) to “fruit for God” (v.4) is the eschatological reversal the resurrection makes possible, and the “newness of the Spirit” (v.6) is the new-covenant law written on the heart (Jeremiah 31:33).

verses.note: “Do you not know, brothers — for I speak to those who know the law — that the law has dominion over a person only as long as he lives?” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note Paul appeals to a maxim his hearers already grant: law binds only the living, and death dissolves its claim. puritan Henry and reformed Calvin see the purpose at once — the principle is laid down to prove, by the marriage figure of vv.2–3, that the believer who has died with Christ is freed from the law's rule.

The illustration: the married woman is bound by law to her husband while he lives, but freed from that law when he dies. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the exact legal force — dedetai, a settled bond — of which his death is the sole and sufficient dissolver. eastern Theodoret already hears the application: the believer is like the woman, freed by a death to be joined to another, even Christ.

The figure completed, with its two verdicts: married to another while her husband lives she is an adulteress, but freed by his death she is no adulteress in marrying again. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the death of the husband alone removes the charge and dissolves the bond. eastern Theodoret draws the parallel tight: only death frees from the marriage law, as only death — the believer's own, with Christ — frees him from the law to a new union.

The application, and the surprise: you also died to the law through the body of Christ, that you might belong to another, to him who was raised from the dead. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the means — the crucified body of Christ, by union with whose death the believer died to the law's claim. reformed Calvin marks the end: the new marriage is fruitful, its purpose “that we should bear fruit for God” — holiness and good works flowing from union with the risen Lord.

The old state in review: when we were in the flesh, sinful passions, aroused by the law, worked in our members to bear fruit for death. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read “in the flesh” as the unrenewed, carnal state before conversion; the law, by forbidding, actually excited the rebellious heart. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the grim harvest — “fruit unto death,” the exact opposite of the fruit unto God of v.4.

“But now we are released from the law, having died to that which held us captive, so that we serve in the new way of the Spirit and not the old way of the written code.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the discharge (katērgēthēmen) — the bond dissolved by death. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret describe the new service: inward, willing, living obedience wrought by the Spirit, over against the dead external bondage of the letter — a transformation the whole of Romans 8 will unfold.

witnesses.point: Paul argues from a legal maxim his hearers grant — law binds only the living — and applies it: the believer, dead with Christ, has passed out of the law's jurisdiction.
The purpose of the whole figure is the fruitful marriage of the soul to the risen Christ; freed from the law by death, the believer bears fruit unto God.
As only death frees the widow to a new husband, so only death with Christ frees the believer from the law to be joined to the risen Lord.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.point: The analogy is not a strict allegory — the point is simply that death dissolves the law's claim; Paul frames it around the believer's death with Christ, not the death of the law itself.

1.application: Freedom from the law is not freedom from obedience but a change of husband. The question is no longer “what does the code require?” but “what does love for the risen Christ, to whom I now belong, bring forth?” The same righteousness the law demanded but could not produce, the Spirit now grows from within — fruit, not compliance.

7.exposition: Having said the law arouses sinful passions (v.5), Paul must forestall the obvious slander: is the law itself sin? By no means (v.7). The law is not the criminal but the searchlight — Paul would not have known coveting to be sin had the commandment not said, “You shall not covet.” Yet sin is cunning: it seized the very commandment as a base of operations, and in the forbidding found its provocation, producing all manner of covetousness (v.8). The narrative that follows is almost the story of Eden retold in the first person — “I was alive apart from the law; the commandment came; sin sprang to life; I died” (vv.9–11). The verdict clears the law entirely: it is holy, righteous, and good (v.12). If it issued in death, that was sin's doing, so that sin — exposed by its abuse of what is good — might be shown to be sin beyond all measure (v.13).
7.original_language: The test case is the tenth word of the Decalogue: ouk epithymēseis (“you shall not covet,” Exodus 20:17), unique among the commandments in forbidding not an act but an inward epithymia (“desire, craving”) — which is exactly why Paul chooses it to expose sin's interior operation. Twice sin is said to take aphormē (v.8, v.11) — a military term for a base of operations, a bridgehead from which to launch attack. And in v.11 sin exēpatēsen me (“deceived, thoroughly beguiled me”) — the very verb used of the serpent's deceiving of Eve. The climax, kath' hyperbolēn hamartōlos (v.13), is “sinful beyond all measure,” sin shown hyperbolically for what it is.
7.historical_context: The vocabulary of vv.8–11 deliberately echoes the Fall: a commandment given, sin “seizing an opportunity,” a deception, and death (Genesis 3:1–6) — the serpent using the prohibition (“you shall not eat”) to produce the very transgression it forbade. The autobiographical “I” is one of the most contested questions in Pauline studies; in 7:7–13 the past tense suits a retrospective narrative — whether of Paul's own awakening, of Adam, or of Israel receiving the law at Sinai — which then gives way to the present-tense agony of 7:14–25.
7.christ: This section is the definitive account of why the law cannot save: it can diagnose sin but not cure it, name the disease but not heal it. That limitation is precisely the gap the gospel fills. What the law “could not do, weakened by the flesh,” God did by sending his own Son (Romans 8:3) — the one “born under the law” (Galatians 4:4) in whom its full demand was met without sin seizing its opportunity. Where Adam's commandment brought death to all, Christ's obedience brings life to all who are united to him.

verses.note: “Is the law sin? By no means! Yet I would not have known sin except through the law.” grammatical-historical Barnes and puritan Henry note the natural cavil arising from vv.5–6, which Paul indignantly rejects. Its office is to reveal, not produce: he had not known coveting to be sin but for “You shall not covet.” reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note that this tenth commandment (Exodus 20:17) reaches past the deed to the desiring heart, exposing inward sin the outward law never touched.

“But sin, seizing an opportunity through the commandment, produced in me every kind of covetousness; for apart from the law, sin lies dead.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that sin, the indwelling principle, made the commandment its aphormē — a base of operations — the mere forbidding of a thing inflaming the corrupt heart to crave it. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret gloss “sin was dead” as dormant, not non-existent: unroused and unrecognized until the law stirred and named it.

“I was once alive apart from the law, but when the commandment came, sin came alive and I died.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read this of a former, false security — the self “alive” only in its unawakened complacency — shattered when the commandment came home in its spiritual force. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note that the law, entering in power, slays self-righteous complacency, rousing sin and bringing the sentence of death to the conscience.

The paradox stated: the very commandment that promised life proved to be death to me. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the reversal — the law was ordained to give life (“do this and live”), yet through sin it became an instrument of death, not by its own fault but through human corruption. reformed Calvin lays the blame squarely: the good law, meeting a corrupt heart, issues in death, and the fault is sin's alone.

“For sin, seizing an opportunity through the commandment, deceived me and through it killed me.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that exēpatēsen (“deceived”) is the serpent's word (Genesis 3:13) — sin promising pleasure or freedom and delivering ruin. eastern Theodoret and reformed Calvin hear the Edenic pattern beneath: as the serpent beguiled Eve through the command, so sin takes the holy commandment as its occasion, deceives, and slays.

The full acquittal: the law is holy, and the commandment holy and righteous and good. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the conclusion — the fault for death lying wholly in sin, Paul praises the commandment in a threefold commendation: holy in nature, righteous in requirement, good in design. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the completeness of the vindication: whatever death the law occasioned is sin's doing; the law itself remains wholly excellent.

“Did that which is good bring death to me? By no means! It was sin, that it might be shown to be sin, working death in me through what is good — that sin through the commandment might become sinful beyond measure.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note it was sin, not the law, that wrought death. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret name the purpose: sin is unmasked precisely by perverting the good — the law's holy office being to expose the sheer, immeasurable enormity of sin.

witnesses.point: The law is not sin but its searchlight: sin seized the commandment as a base of operations, so that the fault lies wholly in sin, never in the holy law.
The good law, meeting a corrupt heart, issues in death — but the guilt is sin's; the law is fully acquitted, holy, righteous, and good.
The commandment against coveting reaches past the outward act to the inward desire, revealing a whole realm of sin the letter of the law never exposed.
Apart from the law sin lay dormant and unrecognized; the commandment did not create it but roused and named it, so that it might appear exceedingly sinful.

external.name: James D.G. Dunn
external.point: The language of sin “coming alive,” deceiving, and killing deliberately echoes the Eden narrative, so the “I” speaks in solidarity with Adam and all humanity, not merely as an autobiography.

7.application: Here is the reason moral instruction alone never transforms a life: the problem is not that we do not know what is right but that the knowing can inflame the very thing it forbids. Sin is rebellion, not ignorance. The commandment reveals sin as sin; only grace addresses sin as a power — which is why the answer to Romans 7 cannot be a better law, but a stronger Savior.

14.exposition: The tense changes, and with it the temperature: from the past-tense narrative of 7:7–13 to a present-tense agony that has no equal in the New Testament for psychological candor. The law is spiritual; the “I” is fleshly, sold under sin (v.14). What follows is the anatomy of a divided self: doing the very thing it hates (vv.15–17), willing the good it cannot perform (vv.18–20), discovering a “law” by which evil lies always to hand (vv.21–23). The inner being delights in God's law even as another law wars in the members and takes the self captive. The build ends in a cry — “Wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me?” (v.24) — and the cry is answered not with a technique but with a name: “Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord!” (v.25). The summary that closes the chapter is deliberately unresolved — mind serving God's law, flesh serving sin's — leaving the full deliverance for Romans 8.
14.original_language: The passage is dominated by an emphatic, insistent egō (“I,” “I myself”) — twenty-some first-person verbs hammering the same divided self. In v.14 the “I” is sarkinos (“made of flesh,” fleshly-material) and pepramenos hypo tēn hamartian (“sold as a slave under sin”). The governing word is sarx (“flesh”) — not the physical body as such, but human nature as it stands in weakness and rebellion, the base from which sin operates; “in me, that is, in my flesh, nothing good dwells” (v.18). The cry of v.24 is talaipōros egō anthrōpos (“wretched, worn-out man that I am”), longing for rescue from tou sōmatos tou thanatou toutou — “the body of this death.”
14.historical_context: The shift to the present tense is the storm-center of the debate: does the “I” describe the believer's ongoing conflict, or the person under the law apart from Christ? The pastoral tradition — Augustine (after 397), Luther, Calvin — reads the regenerate believer, and among moderns Cranfield and Dunn continue that line (the mature Christian whose very anguish marks the Spirit's work; the believer caught in the eschatological tension of the “already and not yet”). Others — Moo and Schreiner — argue it depicts the person under the law of Moses, unregenerate, viewed from the vantage of the gospel; and Wright hears the corporate voice of Israel at Sinai reliving Adam's trespass. The “body of this death” (v.24) may allude to the ancient punishment of chaining a murderer to his victim's corpse until decomposition killed him — a vivid image of being bound to mortality and sin.
14.christ: The climax turns on vv.24–25: to the question “Who will deliver me from this body of death?” the answer is a person, not a program — “Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord!” The risen Jesus is the one who has already done what the law demanded (Romans 8:3) and conquered what sin produced, and who now communicates his victory to those in him. The “body of death” from which rescue is sought is the mortal body still under sin's pull; Christ's resurrection body is the firstfruits of the transformation that will finally deliver believers (Romans 8:23; Philippians 3:21). The thanksgiving comes before the full explanation of Romans 8 because it rests not on present experience but on an accomplished work.

verses.note: “We know that the law is spiritual, but I am fleshly, sold under sin.” eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical Barnes note the law is spiritual — from the Spirit, a teacher of virtue — so the fault lies not in the holy law but in the carnal disciple. Here the great debate over the passage opens. puritan Henry frames the controversy exactly: some take the conflict of the convinced-but-unregenerate soul, others of the renewed-but-imperfect believer. reformed Calvin and Gill (with Augustine's mature view) hold the regenerate; eastern Chrysostom and the wesleyan-arminian Wesley lean to the unregenerate under the law or Paul before conversion.

“I do not understand my own actions: I do not do what I want, but the very thing I hate.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the divided self acting against its own settled judgment — open war between will and deed. The schools read it by their view of the “I”: reformed Calvin sees the believer grieving over indwelling sin's resistance to the renewed will; the wesleyan-arminian and eastern readers see the convinced sinner's helpless struggle under conviction.

“Now if I do what I do not want, I agree with the law, that it is good.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the fine inference: the very reluctance proves the will sides with the law, confirming the goodness already declared in v.12. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret add that the inward conflict itself vindicates the holy law — conscience and commandment siding together against the sinful deed.

“So now it is no longer I who do it, but sin that dwells within me.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the careful distinction between the true self — the renewed will, or the better judgment — and the sin that does the evil; not excusing self, but locating the enemy. The schools divide: reformed Calvin sees the believer disowning the sin that wars against his renewed self; the wesleyan-arminian and eastern read the convicted sinner recognizing sin's power, with care not to excuse himself.

“I know that nothing good dwells in me, that is, in my flesh; for I have the desire to do right, but not the ability.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear the total depravity of the sarx: no good in the unrenewed nature, the will to good present but the power to perform it lacking. The schools read it variously: reformed Calvin sees the believer's renewed will yet unable, through remaining flesh, to perform perfectly; puritan Henry and the wesleyan-arminian read the helplessness of the convicted soul.

“For I do not do the good I want, but the evil I do not want is what I keep on doing.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the deliberate restatement of v.15 for emphasis — the persistent gap between desire and performance. reformed Calvin sees the believer's grief at falling short of his renewed desire; the wesleyan-arminian and eastern read the bondage of the convicted soul, unaided by grace.

“Now if I do what I do not want, it is no longer I who do it, but sin that dwells within me.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the emphatic return to v.17: the doing of the unwilled evil proves an alien power at work, distinct from the willing self. reformed Calvin sees the believer disowning the indwelling sin; the wesleyan-arminian reads the convicted sinner acknowledging sin's mastery.

“So I find it to be a law that when I want to do right, evil lies close at hand.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that “law” here is not the Mosaic law but a fixed inward principle or rule: evil is ever at hand the moment the will turns to good. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret call it the abiding law of the conflict — whether the believer's remaining corruption or the convicted sinner's bondage.

“For I delight in the law of God, in my inner being.” Here the debate sharpens. reformed Calvin and Gill hold that genuine delight in God's law after the inward man is the mark of the regenerate — only the renewed heart truly delights in the law (cf. Psalm 1) — which strongly favors the believer view. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke counters that even a conscientious unregenerate Jew may approve and delight in the law (Isaiah 58:2), so the word alone cannot settle it.

“But I see another law in my members, warring against the law of my mind and making me captive to the law of sin.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note a second principle in the members — the law of sin — that campaigns against the law of the mind (the renewed reason of v.22) and takes the self prisoner. reformed Calvin reads the believer's painful experience of remaining sin's power; the wesleyan-arminian Wesley, the convicted sinner's bondage.

“Wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death?” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson call it the heart-rending climax of the conflict — a cry “from the depths of despair,” with the striking image of “the body of this death,” possibly the tyrant's punishment of binding a living man to a corpse, the loathsome burden the self drags about. reformed Calvin sees the believer bewailing his condition and panting for full deliverance while submitting to God; the wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads the convicted sinner, or pre-conversion Paul, at the very point of despair, just before rescue.

“Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord! So then, I myself serve the law of God with my mind, but with my flesh the law of sin.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the cry answered — deliverance comes not from the law but through Christ (some manuscripts read “the grace of God,” Gill, Clarke). The closing summary is left unresolved: the same self serving God with the mind, sin with the flesh. reformed Calvin and Gill hold the believer continues this conflict through life, the deliverance progressive and consummated at last; the wesleyan-arminian Clarke argues the victory of Romans 8 shows the normal Christian state, so chapter 7 describes the state before full deliverance.

witnesses.point: The law is spiritual and blameless; the conflict describes the plight of human nature under the law, apart from the grace that alone can deliver it.

The passage portrays the regenerate believer's lifelong struggle with indwelling sin: the renewed will delights in God's law yet is resisted by the remaining flesh, and full deliverance is progressive.
The bondage described is that of a soul convinced by the law but not yet set free; the deliverance of the next chapter shows that victory, not captivity, is the normal Christian state.
Frames the controversy itself — the conflict is read by some of the convinced-but-unregenerate soul, by others of the renewed-but-imperfect believer — as the hinge on which the whole passage turns.

external.name: C.E.B. Cranfield
external.point: Defends the believer reading: the intensity of the conflict is itself a mark of grace, since only the person in whom the Spirit is at work feels sin's remaining power this acutely — the more mature the Christian, the sharper the struggle.

14.application: Whatever one decides about the “I,” the passage refuses two lies: that the Christian life is effortless triumph, and that the honest failure of the will means grace has not come. The conflict it names is real, and its answer is not a stronger effort but a person — “through Jesus Christ our Lord.” Discipleship is less about trying harder than about dying more completely, learning to live from Christ's life rather than one's own moral strength, until the body of this death is finally redeemed.

_meta.title: The Law, Sin, and the War Within
reflection: Paul says the believer has “died to the law” and now belongs “to him who was raised from the dead,” to bear fruit for God (v.4). What changes when obedience is reframed not as keeping a code but as the fruit of belonging to the risen Christ?

The commandment did not prevent coveting; it could even provoke it (vv.7–8). Where in your own life have you tried to fix by rules and resolve what only grace can heal, and what would it mean to stop diagnosing and start receiving deliverance?

The cry “Who will deliver me from this body of death?” is answered, “Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord!” (vv.24–25). Where do you most feel the war between the good you want and the evil you do — and how does it change things that the deliverer is a person rather than a program?
